food_name: Kuih Makmur (Malay Ghee Shortbread Cookie with Peanut Filling)
food_subgroup: Festive shortbread cookie — crumbly ghee-based shell filled with sweetened ground peanut paste, dusted with icing sugar; traditional Hari Raya Aidilfitri kuih

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Kuih Makmur. Closest parent is shortbread cookie (FOODON:03301826). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and cookies — closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor for a ghee-based shortbread cookie. No Kuih Makmur-specific code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a Kuih Makmur entry. A000E (Biscuits and cookies) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kuih Makmur or Malay festive shortbread cookies as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Kuih Makmur is 2 pieces (approximately 30g), providing approximately 147 kcal, 8.4g fat, and 16.5g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Kuih Makmur — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (MyFCD) and Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database for Kuih Makmur. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values represent the composite traditional recipe (toasted flour, ghee, icing sugar shell; sweetened ground peanut filling).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index estimation for ghee-based shortbread cookie with peanut filling — analogous to high-fat shortbread biscuit entries in the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kuih Makmur in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~40 (low) is estimated by analogy to high-fat shortbread biscuits (GI 40–55 range in the Sydney GI Database). The very high ghee content (28g fat/100g, 14g saturated fat/100g) dramatically slows gastric emptying and carbohydrate absorption, suppressing the glycemic response despite the icing sugar coating and sweetened peanut filling. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (40 × 16.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.6 (low). Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat from ghee (14g/100g); low sodium (50mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low (15mg per 30g serving) and does not directly raise blood pressure, but the very high saturated fat content (4.2g per 30g serving) is a concern for hypertension patients with concurrent dyslipidaemia — a common metabolic syndrome cluster — and portion moderation is advised, particularly during Hari Raya open house visits where social eating volume can reach 6–10 pieces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~40) per serving but high caloric density and social portion risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kuih Makmur has a low GI (~40) and a manageable glycemic load of 6.6 per 2-piece serving (30g) due to the fat-slowing effect of ghee, making a single serving acceptable for T2DM; however, the primary clinical risk is social eating volume during Hari Raya — consuming 10 pieces (150g) delivers ~33g carbohydrate and a GL of ~22 (moderate-high), and 735 kcal, so patients should be counselled on caloric density and portion limits rather than GI alone.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (14g/100g) and trans fat (0.3g/100g) from ghee; high caloric density (490 kcal/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The very high saturated fat content (14g/100g, 4.2g per 30g serving) and presence of trans fat (0.3g/100g) from ghee are the primary cardiovascular concerns; patients with established CVD or dyslipidaemia should strictly limit intake to 1–2 pieces per occasion, as 6–10 pieces consumed at a Hari Raya open house can deliver 8.4–21g saturated fat — substantially exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of less than 13g saturated fat for a 2000 kcal diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat associated with increased systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The very high saturated fat from ghee (14g/100g) is associated with increased systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; peanut filling provides beneficial Vitamin E (2mg/100g) and niacin (2.5mg/100g), but the overall fat-calorie density warrants portion moderation for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Makmur (meaning 'prosperous cookie' in Malay-Arabic; Chinese: 满福饼, Mǎn Fú Bǐng) is one of Singapore's and Malaysia's most beloved Hari Raya Aidilfitri festive cookies — a small, dome-shaped, melt-in-mouth shortbread filled with sweet ground roasted peanut paste and generously dusted with icing sugar. The shell is made from toasted plain flour blended with generous amounts of ghee (clarified butter), which gives the cookie its signature crumbly, melt-on-the-tongue texture. The filling is sweetened ground roasted peanuts, sometimes with added sugar and a touch of vanilla. The cookie is baked until just set, then rolled in icing sugar while still warm. Nutritionally, Kuih Makmur is calorie-dense at 490 kcal/100g, with 28g fat (14g saturated fat from ghee) and 55g carbohydrate per 100g. It presents a clinically interesting paradox: despite its obviously sweet, white-dusted appearance, it has a LOW glycemic index (~40) because the very high ghee fat content dramatically slows gastric emptying and carbohydrate absorption. The primary clinical concern is not GI but caloric and saturated fat density — a social Hari Raya visit involving 6–10 pieces (60–150g) can deliver 300–735 kcal and 8.4–21g saturated fat from kuih alone. Sodium is low at 50mg/100g. The peanut filling contributes protein (8g/100g), niacin (2.5mg/100g), Vitamin E (2mg/100g), and phosphorus (100mg/100g). Culturally appropriate counselling for Hari Raya should acknowledge the low GI, then focus on caloric density, saturated fat load, and social portion moderation. 'Makmur' means prosperous or flourishing in Arabic and Malay, reflecting the celebratory significance of this cookie during Eid al-Fitr festivities across the Malay Muslim community in Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database — both approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2. The food is a well-documented traditional Malay festive cookie with consistent recipe composition across sources. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on high-fat shortbread biscuit analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kuih Makmur not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-13; total fat and saturated fat are confirmed. water_g set to -1: moisture content for Kuih Makmur not reported in the primary sources consulted. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and MyFCD; this is a baked flour-ghee-peanut product with no vitamin C-contributing ingredients. trans_fat_g 0.3 is reported from ghee content (naturally occurring ruminant trans fats, not industrial partially hydrogenated oils). GI ~40 is an analogous estimate from high-fat shortbread biscuit data in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kuih Makmur exists in the published literature as of 2026. FoodEx2 code A000E (Biscuits and cookies) is the closest defensible parent category; no Kuih Makmur-specific FoodEx2 code exists. Hindi local name (कुइह माकमुर) is a phonetic transliteration; no established Hindi culinary term exists for this Malay-specific festive cookie.
